title: dnsmasq plugin is killed for rooted containers when user session is closed
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

After starting a rooted container over a podman network using sudo, name resolution only works while the session of the user who called sudo is running. This happens because dnsmasq is run under the session of the user who called and not on a slice under machine.slice (like conmon does).

If a rooted container tries to resolve a name of another rooted container on the same network after the session of the user  is closed, it will fail since dnsmasq will be killed by systemd.

I don't believe that for rooted containers this should happen. There is a workaround of enabling lingering at loginctl of every user that could launch podman which is impracticable.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Login to a machine.

2. Launch two rooted containers running bash over the same network using sudo (detached).

3. Logout

4. Wait for session cleanup by systemd.

5. Login again

6. Attach to any of the running containers.

7. Try to resolve the name of the other container.

**Describe the results you received:**

The container will not be able to resolve the IP of the other container.

**Describe the results you expected:**

The container should be able to continue resolving IP of other containers.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

For some reason, dnsmasq is trapped on the user session despite the fact that both containers and network were created using sudo.

**Output of `podman version`:**

Version:      3.0.1
API Version:  3.0.0
Go Version:   go1.15.8
Built:        Fri Feb 19 13:56:17 2021
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide?**

No (only checked Troubleshooting, but have not found any issues like this nor notes of fix on release notes.

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

Physical

comments.body: @Luap99 @mheon @giuseppe wdyt? linger is sort of our answer here no?

@baude This is for Podman launched as root via sudo, so I don't know if linger is the answer - that's a reasonable response for rootless, but here you'd expect the dnsmasq to run in root's context.

I do think the rearchitecture of dnsmasq we were discussing will resolve this.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

@adamoa Are you still seeing this?

@baude @Luap99 @mheon We sort have left this one hanging,  Any thoughts on fixing.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

@adamoa hi, are you still seeing this issue?

@Luap99 @baude @mheon with the new network stack in the works, do we plan to tackle this? If not, let's close the issue.
This is definitely going to be addressed in the new stack.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
netavark and podman 4.x will fix this.
